label: distinct

a.name: python-logging
a.description: Configure Python's standard logging module with handlers, formatters, and levels
a.body:
## Overview

The logging module provides a hierarchical logger tree. Configure root once, then use logging.getLogger(__name__) in each module for namespaced output.

## Handlers

Attach handlers — StreamHandler, FileHandler, RotatingFileHandler — to route log records. Formatters control the output layout.

b.name: rust-cargo-workspaces
b.description: Organize Rust multi-crate projects with Cargo workspaces and shared dependencies
b.body:
## Overview

A Cargo workspace is a set of crates sharing one Cargo.lock and target directory. The top-level Cargo.toml declares members but owns no source itself.

## Benefits

Shared lock files keep dependency versions aligned across crates, and compilation artifacts are cached once across the whole workspace.

note: Python logging vs Rust Cargo — different language, different domain.

label: distinct

a.name: docker-compose
a.description: Define multi-container Docker apps with docker-compose.yml and orchestrate them locally
a.body:
## Overview

Compose describes a stack of services, networks, and volumes in one YAML file. docker compose up starts the whole graph; docker compose down tears it down.

## Use cases

Local development stacks, CI integration tests, and small single-host deployments.

b.name: graphql-resolvers
b.description: Implement GraphQL resolvers mapping schema fields to data sources with batching
b.body:
## Overview

A resolver is a function returning the value for one schema field. Resolvers compose into a tree matching the query shape; DataLoader batches and caches repeated lookups.

## Pattern

Keep resolvers thin — delegate heavy work to service functions and use DataLoader to avoid N+1 database hits across nested fields.

note: Container orchestration vs GraphQL query resolution.

label: distinct

a.name: react-context-api
a.description: Propagate values down React trees with Context to avoid prop drilling
a.body:
## Overview

React.createContext returns a Provider and Consumer pair. Wrap a subtree in the Provider, and any descendant can read the value via useContext.

## When to use it

Context suits infrequently changing global values — theme, current user, locale. Avoid it for fast-changing state that would re-render the whole subtree.

b.name: postgres-vacuum
b.description: Reclaim dead tuples and update statistics with PostgreSQL VACUUM and ANALYZE
b.body:
## Overview

Postgres uses MVCC; updates and deletes leave dead tuples until VACUUM removes them. ANALYZE refreshes planner statistics so query plans stay accurate.

## Autovacuum

The autovacuum daemon runs both automatically based on per-table thresholds. Tune autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor on hot tables to vacuum more often.

note: React state management vs Postgres MVCC maintenance.

label: distinct

a.name: k8s-ingress
a.description: Expose Kubernetes services externally through Ingress resources and a controller
a.body:
## Overview

An Ingress object maps HTTP hostnames and paths to Services. An Ingress controller — nginx, Traefik, HAProxy — watches Ingress resources and programs the actual proxy.

## TLS

Attach a TLS block referencing a Secret containing certificate and key to serve HTTPS for the specified hosts.

b.name: jest-snapshot-testing
b.description: Capture rendered component trees as Jest snapshots to catch unintended changes
b.body:
## Overview

toMatchSnapshot serializes a value into a .snap file on first run and compares on every subsequent run. Mismatches fail the test until the snapshot is updated.

## Caveats

Snapshots decay — large snapshots get rubber-stamped in reviews. Keep them small and focused on structural output, not styling details.

note: Kubernetes ingress vs Jest snapshot testing.

label: distinct

a.name: terraform-state-backend
a.description: Store Terraform state in a remote S3 backend with DynamoDB lock table for safety
a.body:
## Overview

Remote state decouples Terraform from any single workstation. S3 holds the .tfstate file; a DynamoDB table provides the lock that prevents concurrent applies.

## Bootstrapping

Use a small local-state Terraform project to create the bucket, lock table, and IAM policies, then migrate the main project's state into them with terraform init -migrate-state.

b.name: vue-template-refs
b.description: Access DOM nodes and child components in Vue templates via ref attributes
b.body:
## Overview

Attaching ref='name' to an element in a Vue template exposes it as this.$refs.name in the component instance. In the Composition API, use const node = ref(null) and assign ref='node' in the template.

## Timing

Refs are populated after the component mounts. Access them inside onMounted rather than during setup to avoid reading before they exist.

note: Terraform remote state vs Vue DOM refs.

label: distinct

a.name: aws-lambda-layers
a.description: Share dependencies across AWS Lambda functions with Layers for smaller function packages
a.body:
## Overview

A Lambda layer is a .zip of shared libraries mounted at /opt in the function runtime. Functions reference layers by ARN; one layer can be used by many functions.

## Limits

A function can attach up to five layers. The total unzipped size — function plus layers — must stay under the 250 MB Lambda limit.

b.name: webpack-bundle-analyzer
b.description: Visualize Webpack bundle composition with webpack-bundle-analyzer to find bloat
b.body:
## Overview

webpack-bundle-analyzer generates an interactive treemap of a production bundle, showing each module's size relative to the whole. Run it after a build to spot the top contributors.

## Workflow

Add the plugin with analyzerMode: 'static' to produce an HTML report, open it in a browser, and chase the largest rectangles until the bundle fits its budget.

note: Lambda packaging vs Webpack bundle analysis.

label: distinct

a.name: git-worktrees
a.description: Check out multiple branches simultaneously with git worktrees sharing one .git directory
a.body:
## Overview

git worktree add creates an additional working directory tied to the same repository. Each worktree has its own checkout, so you can build one branch while editing another without stashing.

## Cleanup

Remove a worktree with git worktree remove; prune stale entries with git worktree prune when a directory was deleted manually.

b.name: css-flexbox-layout
b.description: Lay out one-dimensional component rows and columns using CSS flexbox
b.body:
## Overview

flexbox handles one-dimensional layout. display: flex on a container lines up its children; justify-content spaces them along the main axis and align-items along the cross axis.

## Children

flex-grow, flex-shrink, and flex-basis — combined as the flex shorthand — control how each child takes unused space or gives up space when the container shrinks.

note: Git worktrees vs CSS flexbox layout.

a.name: mongodb-aggregation
a.description: Shape MongoDB result sets with aggregation pipeline stages like $match and $group
a.body:
## Overview

The aggregation framework is a pipeline of stages. $match filters, $group aggregates, $project shapes output, $lookup joins. Each stage feeds into the next.

## Performance

Place $match as early as possible so indexes apply; avoid large $lookup or $unwind operations on cold fields.

b.name: rust-traits
b.description: Define shared behavior in Rust with traits and implement them on concrete types
b.body:
## Overview

A trait declares a set of method signatures. Types implement the trait with an impl Trait for Type block, gaining the trait's methods and letting generic code dispatch on the trait.

## Bounds

Generic functions constrain type parameters with trait bounds: fn f<T: Clone + Debug>(x: T). The compiler rejects calls where T doesn't implement every bound.

a.name: typescript-generics
a.description: Write reusable TypeScript functions and types parameterized by generic type variables
a.body:
## Overview

TypeScript generics let a function or type accept a placeholder type: function identity<T>(x: T): T { return x }. Callers pass a concrete type or let inference choose.

## Constraints

Express requirements on T with extends: function first<T extends { id: string }>(x: T) — the caller must pass something with an id property.

b.name: docker-volumes
b.description: Persist container data with Docker named volumes or bind mounts
b.body:
## Overview

Docker volumes persist data across container restarts. Named volumes are managed by Docker under /var/lib/docker/volumes; bind mounts map a host path into the container.

## Choosing one

Named volumes are portable and easier to back up; bind mounts are the right call for source code during local development.

a.name: fastapi-dependency-injection
a.description: Compose request-scoped resources in FastAPI with the Depends dependency injection system
a.body:
## Overview

FastAPI's Depends resolves a callable per request, caches its result within that request, and supplies it to any handler that declares it. Dependencies can themselves depend on other dependencies.

## Patterns

Express database sessions, auth context, and pagination parameters as dependencies so handlers stay focused on business logic and tests can override any dependency cleanly.

b.name: jenkins-pipelines
b.description: Define Jenkins CI/CD pipelines as declarative Jenkinsfile scripts with stages and steps
b.body:
## Overview

A declarative Jenkinsfile defines a pipeline with stages and steps. Each stage runs in order; steps inside a stage execute sequentially.

## Agents

Pipelines specify an agent (any, docker, label) per pipeline or per stage. Shared libraries let multiple projects reuse pipeline code.

a.name: django-admin-customize
a.description: Customize the Django admin with ModelAdmin subclasses to tune list views and filters
a.body:
## Overview

Registering a model with a ModelAdmin subclass controls how it appears in the admin: list_display sets columns, list_filter adds sidebar filters, search_fields enables the search box.

## Inlines

Inline admin classes embed related models inside a parent's change page — TabularInline for table layout, StackedInline for card layout.

b.name: android-jetpack-compose
b.description: Build Android UIs declaratively with Jetpack Compose composable functions
b.body:
## Overview

Compose lets you describe UI as @Composable functions that emit widgets. State objects drive recomposition — the framework re-runs the functions that depend on changed state.

## State hoisting

Keep composables stateless when possible and lift state to a parent that owns it, passing value plus onValueChange downwards.

a.name: nginx-reverse-proxy
a.description: Configure nginx as a reverse proxy with upstream blocks, proxy_pass, and TLS termination
a.body:
## Overview

nginx can front-end any backend by proxy_pass'ing to an upstream. Define the backend pool in an upstream block, then reference it in a server block listening on 80/443.

## TLS

Terminate TLS at nginx with ssl_certificate and ssl_certificate_key directives; forward plaintext to the backend over a trusted network.

b.name: swift-concurrency
b.description: Use Swift's async/await and actors for structured concurrency and data isolation
b.body:
## Overview

Swift async functions suspend instead of blocking; await resumes when the awaited task completes. Actors serialize access to their mutable state, preventing data races without locks.

## Tasks

Task { ... } launches unstructured concurrent work; async let binds child tasks inside a parent's scope so cancellation propagates automatically.

label: distinct

a.name: prometheus-scrape-config
a.description: Configure Prometheus scrape targets with static configs and service discovery
a.body:
## Overview

Prometheus pulls metrics from HTTP endpoints listed in scrape_configs. Each job has a scrape_interval and either static_configs or a service-discovery mechanism like Kubernetes SD.

## Relabeling

relabel_configs rewrite target labels before scraping — typical use is dropping noisy pods or promoting Kubernetes labels into Prometheus labels.

b.name: git-reflog-recovery
b.description: Recover lost commits using git reflog after a botched reset or rebase
b.body:
## Overview

git reflog records every movement of HEAD and branch tips locally, even those that are otherwise unreachable. Every commit you made is findable for 90 days by default.

## Recovery

Find the lost commit's hash via git reflog, then git branch rescue <hash> to attach a branch to it, bringing it back into normal history.

note: Prometheus scraping vs git reflog recovery.

a.name: kotlin-coroutines
a.description: Write asynchronous Kotlin code with coroutines, suspend functions, and structured concurrency
a.body:
## Overview

suspend functions can pause and resume without blocking a thread. launch and async start coroutines inside a CoroutineScope; a scope's cancellation propagates to all its children.

## Dispatchers

Dispatchers.IO is for blocking IO, Dispatchers.Default for CPU work, Dispatchers.Main for UI. withContext switches dispatcher for a block of code.

b.name: spring-security-oauth
b.description: Secure Spring Boot applications with Spring Security OAuth2 resource server support
b.body:
## Overview

Spring Security's oauth2-resource-server validates bearer tokens on every request. Configure it with jwt() and point it at an issuer URL; Spring downloads the public keys automatically.

## Method security

@PreAuthorize annotations apply scope or role checks at the method level; combine with @EnableMethodSecurity on a configuration class.

label: distinct

a.name: ansible-playbooks
a.description: Automate server configuration with Ansible playbooks grouped into roles and tasks
a.body:
## Overview

A playbook is a YAML list of plays; each play targets a group of hosts and runs an ordered set of tasks. Roles bundle related tasks, templates, and files into a directory layout.

## Idempotence

Ansible modules aim for idempotence — running a playbook twice converges the target state rather than making duplicate changes.

b.name: sql-window-functions
b.description: Aggregate over partitions of query results with SQL window functions like ROW_NUMBER and LAG
b.body:
## Overview

Window functions compute values across a window of related rows without collapsing the result set. OVER (PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY ...) defines the window.

## Common functions

ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK for ordering; LAG and LEAD for neighbor lookups; SUM/AVG/COUNT for running aggregates inside a partition.

note: Ansible config management vs SQL window functions.

label: distinct

a.name: elasticsearch-mappings
a.description: Define Elasticsearch index mappings with field types, analyzers, and dynamic templates
a.body:
## Overview

An index mapping declares the schema of its documents: text vs keyword vs numeric types, analyzers for tokenization, multi-fields for multiple representations of one source value.

## Dynamic templates

dynamic_templates match newly-seen fields by pattern and assign a mapping — useful for wide event shapes with unknown future fields.

b.name: bash-signal-trap
b.description: Handle signals like SIGINT and SIGTERM in bash scripts with the trap builtin
b.body:
## Overview

trap 'cleanup' EXIT runs the cleanup function when the script exits for any reason. Catch SIGINT or SIGTERM separately to react to interactive ^C or docker stop.

## Patterns

Wrap a long-running loop in a trap that clears a flag; the loop tests the flag and exits cleanly instead of being killed mid-iteration.

note: Elasticsearch mappings vs bash signal handling.

a.name: ruby-on-rails-active-record
a.description: Model relational tables with Rails ActiveRecord classes, associations, and validations
a.body:
## Overview

An ActiveRecord model maps one-to-one with a database table. has_many, belongs_to, and has_many through: declare associations; validates_* macros enforce invariants before save.

## Scopes

Named scopes — scope :active, -> { where(active: true) } — produce chainable query fragments that read naturally at call sites.

b.name: gpu-cuda-kernels
b.description: Author CUDA kernels that run on NVIDIA GPUs with thread blocks and shared memory
b.body:
## Overview

A CUDA kernel launches a grid of thread blocks; each block runs on one streaming multiprocessor. threadIdx, blockIdx, and blockDim give each thread its coordinates.

## Shared memory

__shared__ arrays live per-block and stage data from slow global memory. Coalesce global loads and avoid bank conflicts in shared memory for peak throughput.

a.name: cors-handling
a.description: Configure CORS headers on HTTP APIs to permit cross-origin browser requests safely
a.body:
## Overview

Browsers block cross-origin XHR unless the server returns matching CORS headers. Access-Control-Allow-Origin names the allowed origins; preflight OPTIONS requests negotiate allowed methods and headers.

## Credentials

To include cookies, set Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true and specify a concrete origin — wildcards aren't allowed for credentialed requests.

b.name: golang-channels
b.description: Coordinate Go goroutines with channels, select statements, and buffered sends
b.body:
## Overview

Channels pass values between goroutines: ch <- v sends, <-ch receives. Unbuffered channels synchronize sender and receiver; buffered channels decouple them up to the buffer size.

## Select

A select statement waits on multiple channel operations and proceeds with whichever is ready first, letting one goroutine multiplex across several streams.

label: distinct

a.name: rust-async-tokio
a.description: Write async Rust with Tokio's runtime, async/await, and structured tasks
a.body:
## Overview

Tokio is a multi-threaded async runtime for Rust. Async functions return Futures that the runtime polls; tokio::spawn launches a task on the runtime's thread pool.

## IO

Tokio wraps standard IO types — TcpStream, UdpSocket, File — with non-blocking equivalents that suspend via await instead of blocking a thread.

b.name: html-semantic-elements
b.description: Use HTML5 semantic elements like article, section, and nav for accessible document structure
b.body:
## Overview

Semantic elements convey structural meaning screen readers can announce: <nav> for navigation, <main> for primary content, <article> for standalone posts, <section> for thematic groupings.

## Benefit

Semantic markup improves accessibility and SEO compared with a sea of <div> elements, and it communicates intent to future maintainers at zero runtime cost.

note: Tokio async Rust vs semantic HTML.

a.name: influxdb-time-series
a.description: Store and query time-series data in InfluxDB with measurements, tags, and fields
a.body:
## Overview

InfluxDB organizes points into measurements. Tags are indexed metadata used for filtering; fields hold the numeric or string values being measured.

## Retention

Retention policies drop old data automatically — useful for bounded storage. Continuous queries downsample raw points into coarser summaries for long-term retention.

b.name: python-abc-base-classes
b.description: Define abstract base classes in Python with abc.ABC and @abstractmethod
b.body:
## Overview

Inherit from abc.ABC and decorate methods with @abstractmethod to declare abstract methods that subclasses must implement; instantiating a class with unimplemented abstracts raises TypeError.

## Virtual subclasses

Register an unrelated class as a virtual subclass with Base.register(Other) — useful for type-checking pre-existing classes against an ABC without inheritance.

a.name: nats-messaging
a.description: Publish and subscribe with NATS subject-based messaging and JetStream persistence
a.body:
## Overview

NATS is a lightweight pub/sub system keyed on subjects. Publishers emit on a subject; subscribers filter by subject pattern including wildcards. JetStream adds persistent streams and consumer state.

## Patterns

Request-reply via a subject + inbox; queue groups for competing consumers; JetStream consumers for at-least-once delivery.

b.name: css-grid-layout
b.description: Lay out two-dimensional grids with CSS Grid's tracks, template areas, and gap
b.body:
## Overview

display: grid enables a two-dimensional layout. grid-template-columns and grid-template-rows define track sizes; grid-template-areas names regions for visual assignment.

## Responsive

repeat(auto-fit, minmax(200px, 1fr)) produces a responsive grid where column count adjusts to available width without a media query.

a.name: saltstack-states
a.description: Manage server state with SaltStack state files applying idempotent configuration
a.body:
## Overview

A Salt state is a YAML file describing desired state — packages installed, services running, files present. salt '*' state.apply pushes the state to all matched minions.

## Pillar

Pillar data is the per-minion configuration accessible inside state files. Sensitive values live in pillar so they never leak into state files shared across minions.

b.name: regex-lookbehind-lookahead
b.description: Use regex lookbehind and lookahead assertions for zero-width positional matching
b.body:
## Overview

Lookbehind (?<=X) and lookahead (?=X) match a position without consuming characters. Negative variants (?<!X) and (?!X) assert the absence of a pattern.

## Example

(?<=\$)\d+ matches a number immediately preceded by a dollar sign, without including the sign in the match. Useful for extracting inside-context substrings.

label: distinct

a.name: graphql-subscriptions
a.description: Push real-time GraphQL updates to clients with subscriptions over WebSocket
a.body:
## Overview

GraphQL subscriptions deliver push updates over a WebSocket transport. The server publishes events on topics; clients receive matching events as streams.

## Infrastructure

Behind the resolver lives a pub/sub system — Redis, NATS, Kafka — that fans events out to all server instances so every client gets every event regardless of which instance they connect to.

b.name: scala-case-classes
b.description: Model immutable data with Scala case classes, pattern matching, and copy semantics
b.body:
## Overview

A case class gets a public constructor for all fields, equals/hashCode, toString, and a copy method for free. Pattern matching destructures case classes directly.

## Usage

Prefer case classes for algebraic data types and DTOs. The generated copy method is the standard way to produce a modified version without mutation.

note: GraphQL subscriptions vs Scala case classes.

a.name: http-caching-headers
a.description: Control browser and CDN caching with Cache-Control, ETag, and conditional requests
a.body:
## Overview

Cache-Control drives caching behavior: max-age, s-maxage for CDN overrides, stale-while-revalidate for background refresh. ETag plus If-None-Match enables conditional GETs that return 304.

## Strategy

Static assets with hashed filenames can use immutable plus a long max-age. HTML with mutable content needs shorter lifetimes plus ETag revalidation.

b.name: prolog-basics
b.description: Model facts and rules in Prolog and query them with unification-based inference
b.body:
## Overview

A Prolog program is a set of facts and rules. Queries attempt to unify a goal against the database; the engine backtracks through alternatives to find all solutions.

## Lists

Lists are written [H|T] with H the head and T the tail. Recursive predicates process them one element at a time in classic functional style.

a.name: aws-s3-lifecycle
a.description: Transition and expire S3 objects automatically with bucket lifecycle rules
a.body:
## Overview

S3 lifecycle rules shift objects between storage classes and delete them after a given age. Typical progression: Standard -> Standard-IA -> Glacier -> expiration.

## Scoping

Filter rules by prefix, tag, or size so different object families age on different schedules inside one bucket.

b.name: llvm-ir-passes
b.description: Transform LLVM IR with analysis and transformation passes in the compiler pipeline
b.body:
## Overview

LLVM IR is a typed SSA intermediate representation between frontends and backends. Passes walk the IR to either analyze it or rewrite it, composed into a pipeline.

## Writing a pass

Subclass PassInfoMixin and implement run(Module&, ModuleAnalysisManager&). Register the pass with the new pass manager and schedule it in the pipeline.

a.name: helm-chart-basics
a.description: Package Kubernetes manifests as Helm charts with values.yaml and Go templating
a.body:
## Overview

A Helm chart is a directory of templated YAML plus a values.yaml. helm install renders templates with the values and applies the result to the cluster.

## Releases

Helm tracks each install as a named release; upgrades and rollbacks operate on the release rather than raw manifests.

b.name: lisp-macros
b.description: Extend Lisp with macros that operate on code before evaluation
b.body:
## Overview

A Lisp macro is a function that runs at compile time and returns code. Because code is data (S-expressions), macros manipulate program structure directly instead of text.

## Hygiene

Traditional Common Lisp macros require care with gensyms to avoid variable capture; Scheme's syntax-rules and syntax-case provide hygiene automatically.

a.name: oauth-pkce-mobile
a.description: Implement OAuth authorization in mobile apps using PKCE and custom URI schemes
a.body:
## Overview

Mobile apps use the native browser to run the OAuth authorization step, then receive the code via a registered custom URI scheme or universal link. PKCE blocks code interception attacks.

## Secure storage

Access tokens live in the platform keychain (iOS) or keystore (Android), never in plain SharedPreferences or UserDefaults.

b.name: postgres-json-types
b.description: Store and query JSON documents in Postgres with jsonb and operators like -> and @>
b.body:
## Overview

The jsonb column type stores decoded JSON in binary form with indexing support. -> extracts child values, @> tests containment, jsonb_path_query navigates paths.

## Indexing

GIN indexes on jsonb accelerate containment queries; expression indexes on specific paths help point lookups of individual fields.

a.name: elixir-otp-supervisors
a.description: Structure Elixir applications with OTP supervision trees and restart strategies
a.body:
## Overview

An OTP supervisor watches its children and restarts them on crash. Strategies — one_for_one, one_for_all, rest_for_one — control which siblings restart together.

## Let it crash

Elixir favors letting processes crash cleanly and relying on supervisors to restore state, rather than defensive error handling in every process.

b.name: solidity-smart-contracts
b.description: Write Ethereum smart contracts in Solidity with state variables, modifiers, and events
b.body:
## Overview

A Solidity contract is a unit of code deployed to the Ethereum blockchain. State variables persist in storage; functions execute transactions; events emit logs indexable off-chain.

## Safety

Reentrancy guards, checks-effects-interactions ordering, and pull-over-push payments are the baseline defenses against the common attack patterns.

a.name: hasura-permissions
a.description: Secure Hasura GraphQL endpoints with row-level permissions per Postgres role
a.body:
## Overview

Hasura maps GraphQL operations to Postgres queries. Permission rules, defined per role and table, inject WHERE clauses so each role only sees its allowed rows.

## Session variables

Permissions reference session variables like x-hasura-user-id, set by the upstream auth system and injected into the SQL template at request time.

b.name: java-records
b.description: Define concise immutable data carriers in Java 16+ with record types
b.body:
## Overview

A record declares an immutable data class in one line: record Point(int x, int y) {}. The compiler generates the constructor, accessors, equals, hashCode, and toString.

## Compact constructors

A compact constructor runs validation on each field before the generated assignment — useful for invariants that shouldn't be enforced separately by every caller.

a.name: clickhouse-materialized-views
a.description: Pre-aggregate ClickHouse tables with materialized views that update on insert
a.body:
## Overview

A ClickHouse materialized view is an insert-time trigger that writes into a target table after each insert into the source. With SummingMergeTree or AggregatingMergeTree it maintains running aggregates without recomputation.

## Tradeoffs

Materialized views speed up repeated aggregation queries at the cost of additional storage and insert-time CPU. Choose granularity carefully.

b.name: vim-macros
b.description: Record and replay vim command sequences with registers to automate repetitive edits
b.body:
## Overview

Press q<letter> to start recording into register <letter>, perform the sequence, then q to stop. Replay with @<letter>; repeat with @@.

## Scaling up

Prefix with a count like 100@a to apply the macro 100 times, and combine with :g patterns to apply a macro on every line matching a regex.
